consequence.description: Different initial guesses could converge to different local minima, producing materially different volatility surfaces that affect all downstream option pricing and risk calculations

core.when: When setting up pricing engine for any derivative pricing operation
core.action: Explicitly set evaluation date before any pricing call to verify deterministic time-to-expiry calculations

consequence.description: Without explicit evaluation date setup, the system may use default or stale dates causing incorrect time-to-expiry calculations, leading to wrong option prices and Greeks that do not match live trading systems

core.when: When configuring TreeCallableFixedRateBondEngine for Hull-White callable bond pricing
core.action: Use grid_points=40 for the tree discretization — this value balances accuracy against computational cost

consequence.description: Using fewer than 40 grid points causes visible oscillations in OAS sensitivity calculations, leading to inaccurate callable bond pricing that may result in mispriced instruments and trading losses

core.when: When defining interest rate swap instruments for pricing or risk management
core.action: Correctly identify swap direction as Payer or Receiver — Payer pays fixed and receives floating, Receiver receives fixed and pays floating

consequence.description: Misidentifying payer/receiver reverses each cash flow sign, causing P&L attribution to be completely inverted and risk exposure to be misreported by 100%

evidence_refs.summary: 1f2a771 Derived from BD: BD-052

core.when: When configuring CMS coupon pricing engines
core.action: Use GFunctionFactory.Standard implementation per Hagan's formula for consistent CMS coupon calculation

consequence.description: Using non-standard G-function variants produces different CMS adjustments, leading to inconsistent coupon calculations across the system and potential P&L discrepancies in live trading

core.when: When configuring FdmBlackScholesVanillaEngine for finite difference option pricing
core.action: Use xSteps=100 and tSteps=25 for the finite difference discretization — this configuration ensures convergence to within 1bp accuracy for vanilla options across strike and maturity ranges

consequence.description: Using fewer than 100 x-steps or 25 t-steps introduces visible discretization error for long-dated options, causing systematic mispricing that may result in incorrect hedge ratios and trading losses

core.when: When calibrating swaption model parameters using the framework
core.action: Use derivative-free optimization (Nelder-Mead Simplex) with convergence tolerance of 1e-8 — do not substitute with gradient-based methods (Levenberg-Marquardt, quasi-Newton) even if gradients become computable

consequence.description: Gradient-based methods may converge to local minima in the swaption calibration parameter surface, producing incorrect calibrated parameters that cause systematic mispricing of bermudan swaptions across all tenors

evidence_refs.summary: 1f2a771 Derived from BD: BD-071

core.when: When extracting implied volatility from swaption prices using the framework
core.action: Use Newton-bisection hybrid method with 1e-10 tolerance — do not use pure Newton method for speed optimization in well-behaved regions

consequence.description: Pure Newton method can diverge on flat payoff regions where the derivative approaches zero, causing implied volatility extraction to fail or produce unstable results that corrupt all downstream pricing calculations

evidence_refs.summary: 1f2a771 Derived from BD: BD-072

core.when: When pricing callable bonds using the framework's Hull-White tree engine
core.action: Use backward induction on recombining lattice comparing continuation value to exercise value at each node, with convergence within 0.5 basis point

consequence.description: Using semi-analytical approximations for speed-critical applications introduces valuation errors in complex call schedules where early exercise boundary is path-dependent, potentially causing mispriced bonds by several basis points

evidence_refs.summary: 1f2a771 Derived from BD: BD-074

core.when: When constructing zero curve from observed market points using the framework
core.action: Use linear interpolation between observed market points to verify interpolation error stays under 0.1 basis point for standard market tenors

consequence.description: Cubic spline interpolation may introduce spurious oscillations in forward rates that distort discount factors across the curve, causing inconsistent valuation errors that compound in long-dated instruments

evidence_refs.summary: 1f2a771 Derived from BD: BD-075

core.when: When pricing caps and floors using the framework
core.action: Use Black model under lognormal forward rate assumption without checking rate environment — for low rate environments (near zero or negative rates), switch to Normal (Bachelier) model

consequence.description: Black model under lognormal assumption produces unreliable cap prices when forward rates approach zero, systematically mispricing caps in low-rate environments and causing significant P&L variance in live trading

core.action: Verify that zero-coupon swap NPV equals zero at inception (par value) as a sanity check for the pricing engine

consequence.description: Removing or weakening the zero-NPV assertion allows discount/projection curve mismatches to go undetected, producing systematically incorrect swap valuations

core.action: Use 'MaturityStrikeByDeltaGamma' weighting mode rather than 'Naive' mode — Delta-Gamma weighting adjusts for varying instrument sensitivities and provides more stable calibration

core.action: Use binomial tree methods with backward induction for American option pricing — they provide convergence to true value as steps increase and explicit early exercise boundary evolution

core.action: Use Least Squares Monte Carlo (Longstaff-Schwartz) method — estimate continuation value through regression basis functions to determine optimal early exercise for multi-asset path-dependent high-dimensional problems
